Review IKEv2 stats for failure counters — auth failures indicate key/cert mismatch, init failures indicate proposal/reachability issues
Use show crypto ikev2 diagnose error for the most recent failure reason

runbook_fe25624b.name: IPsec SA and Rekey Validation
runbook_fe25624b.description: Diagnose IPsec (Phase 2 / Child SA) failures including SA establishment, transform-set mismatch, crypto ACL mismatch, traffic selectors, rekey failures, and SA lifetime management. Use when IPsec SA is not working or rekey is failing. Run 'IPsec Common Baseline' parent workflow first.
symptoms: IPsec SA lifetime expiring without rekey

cli_commands.purpose: Full IPsec SA details — per-SA SPI values, remaining lifetime (seconds and kilobytes), encaps/decaps/encrypt/decrypt counters, replay protection, rekey status, transform-set, traffic selectors (proxy identities), and error counters (send/recv errors, bad headers)

interpretation_rules.message: IPsec SA remaining lifetime is below 2 minutes — rekey should have already been initiated
interpretation_rules.recommendation: If SA expires without successful rekey, traffic will be dropped until new SA is negotiated. Check rekey counters in show crypto ikev2 stats

interpretation_rules.message: IPsec SA remaining volume-based lifetime is below 1 MB — volume-based rekey should trigger soon
interpretation_rules.recommendation: High-throughput tunnels may hit volume limits before time limits. Verify rekey is succeeding for volume-triggered rekeys

interpretation_rules.message: Anti-replay rollover packets detected — possible out-of-order delivery or replay attack
interpretation_rules.recommendation: Check for asymmetric routing, QoS reordering, or potential replay attack. Consider increasing anti-replay window size

interpretation_rules.message: IPsec send errors detected — packets failed encryption/encapsulation
interpretation_rules.recommendation: Check crypto engine status, MTU issues, or resource constraints

interpretation_rules.message: IPsec receive errors detected — packets failed decryption/decapsulation
interpretation_rules.recommendation: Check for SPI mismatch between peers, corrupted packets, or anti-replay failures

cli_commands.purpose: Summary count of all IPsec SAs — total, active, rekeying, and dead SAs. High dead SA count may indicate rekey or cleanup issues
Display configured IPsec transform-sets — ESP encryption and integrity algorithms, mode (tunnel/transport). Must match peer's transform-set for child SA to establish
Display crypto map entries — peer address, transform-set, ACL for interesting traffic, PFS group, and SA lifetime settings. Only applicable for policy-based VPNs (not tunnel protection mode)
Cross-reference IKEv2 SA detail for child SA rekey timestamps. Shows last rekey time and next rekey scheduled. Essential for validating rekey behavior
Check IKEv2 child SA (CREATE_CHILD) success and failure counters specifically for rekey operations. Non-zero rekey failures indicate systematic rekey issues

interpretation_rules.message: IKEv2 child SA rekey failures detected — IPsec SA rekey is not succeeding
interpretation_rules.recommendation: Rekey failure causes: transform-set mismatch after config change, PFS group mismatch, traffic selector mismatch, or resource exhaustion. Check both peers' configs

cli_commands.purpose: Filter IPsec SAs for a specific peer — useful when multiple tunnels exist. Shows SAs only for the specified peer address
Review IPsec-specific configuration: transform-sets, profiles, lifetime settings (seconds and kilobytes), PFS group, and tunnel protection profile assignments

cli_commands.command: show crypto ipsec sa | include lifetime|rekey
cli_commands.purpose: Quick scan across all IPsec SAs for lifetime and rekey related lines — identifies which SAs are approaching rekey or have recently rekeyed

IPsec internal error counters — surfaces decryption / integrity / SPI-lookup / SA-install errors that the public 'show crypto ipsec sa' counters do not expose. NOTE on Argon (C9350 Silicon One) only the 'error' sub-command of 'show crypto ipsec internal' is registered; the Doppler variants (brief/dual/history/socket/state-machine/timestamp) are NOT supported and will return '% Invalid input'. Always snapshot before AND after the suspected event window — non-zero deltas point at the failure path

List configured IPsec client policies (used by FlexVPN / AnyConnect-IPsec deployments). Output 'No policy exists' is normal for SVTI / crypto-map deployments. For named lookups use 'show crypto ipsec policy name <policy-name>'

Dump every configured IPsec profile (including 'default') — shows IKEv2-profile binding, transform-set list, lifetime (sec & kilobytes), Dualstack, Responder-Only, PFS, and Mixed-mode flags. Catches volume-rekey-disabled or PFS-mismatch surprises before they bite during rekey. For one profile use 'show crypto ipsec profile <name>'

interpretation_rules.message: Kilobyte volume-based rekey is disabled on this profile — only time-based rekey will fire. On high-throughput tunnels this can risk PN rollover before the time-based rekey window
interpretation_rules.recommendation: If the tunnel sustains > ~1 Gbps, consider re-enabling kilobyte volume rekey or enabling ESN to avoid 32-bit ESP sequence rollover (catastrophic with GCM)

cli_commands.purpose: IPsec outbound SA hash table (VESEN / SD-WAN fast-lookup view). Confirms the outbound SA is hashed into the IOS fast-path lookup keyed by local/remote IP and ports. An empty table after a tunnel comes UP indicates the outbound SA was negotiated but never installed in the fast-path — symptom of a software-fallback or PAL install failure

Active SPI table with per-SPI detail — confirms each negotiated SPI is registered for inbound lookup. Cross-check with 'show crypto ipsec sa' SPI values: any SPI present in the SA but missing from the lookup table means the inbound SA is NOT armed in the data-plane and decap will fail

Display the global default IPsec SA lifetime (seconds & kilobytes). Per-profile / per-map values override these defaults; this is the fallback when neither is set. Useful when 'show crypto ipsec sa' shows surprising rekey timing

Display the global default SA idle-timeout. If non-zero, SAs with no traffic for the configured interval are torn down — explains spontaneous tunnel teardowns on quiet tunnels and can be confused with DPD-triggered teardowns

cli_commands.command: show crypto ipsec sa identity detail
cli_commands.purpose: SA table indexed by traffic-selector (proxy ACL) identity with full counters. Critical for multi-SA tunnels where different proxy ACLs / identities map to different SA pairs. Counters are reported per identity — zero encaps/decaps for a specific identity row pinpoints which traffic-selector is failing while siblings are OK

SA table sorted by destination address — fastest way to find every SA pointed at one peer when many tunnels are configured. Complements 'show crypto ipsec sa peer <peer_ip>' (order 6) for full-fan-out audits

cli_commands.command: show crypto ipsec sa interface tunnel {tunnel_id}
cli_commands.purpose: Per-tunnel IPsec SA view — same fields as 'show crypto ipsec sa detail' but scoped to one tunnel interface. Replace {tunnel_id} with the numeric tunnel id (e.g. 100 for Tunnel100). Preferred drill-down once 'show crypto session brief' identifies the suspect tunnel

Per-crypto-map IPsec SA view — relevant for legacy static crypto-map deployments. Replace {crypto_map_tag} with the crypto map name (e.g. 'Tunnel100-head-0' for VTI-on-crypto-map). Skip on pure SVTI deployments

Per-iVRF IPsec SA view — required for VRF-aware IPsec (iVRF != fVRF). Replace {ivrf} with the inside VRF name. Confirms SAs are programmed in the expected VRF; an SA missing in 'sa vrf' but present in 'sa detail' usually means iVRF binding is wrong on the tunnel interface

cli_commands.command: show crypto ipsec sa ipv6 detailed
cli_commands.purpose: IPv6 IPsec SA detailed view — required for v6 SVTI / v6 IKEv2 tunnels. NOTE on Argon the keyword is 'detailed' (not 'detail'). For per-tunnel drill-down use 'show crypto ipsec sa ipv6 interface tunnel <tunnel_id>'. Only relevant when IPv6 is configured on the tunnel — skip otherwise

analysis_points: IPsec SA must have non-zero SPIs for both inbound and outbound
Encaps and decaps counters must be incrementing for active tunnels
Remaining lifetime (seconds) should decrease steadily — rekey initiates at ~90% of configured lifetime by default
Volume-based lifetime (kilobytes) may trigger rekey before time-based lifetime on high-throughput tunnels
Transform-set (ESP encryption + integrity + mode) must match exactly between peers
Crypto ACL / traffic selectors must be mirror images on both peers
PFS group (if configured) must match on both peers — mismatch causes child SA creation failure
CRITICAL: After rekey, old SA should be deleted and new SA installed atomically. If both old and new SAs exist simultaneously for extended period, check for stale SA cleanup issues
Review IKEv2 stats CREATE_CHILD counters — high failure count relative to success count indicates systematic rekey problems
Anti-replay errors may indicate asymmetric routing or packet reordering — not necessarily an attack

ARGON (C9350 Silicon One) DEEP-DIVE: orders 9-21 add C9350-verified drill-downs. Use 'show crypto ipsec internal error' (order 9) to snapshot internal error counters before/after the suspected event (only the 'error' sub-command exists on Argon — the Doppler internal-state-machine variants are NOT registered). Use 'show crypto ipsec profile' (order 11) to detect Kilobyte-Volume-Rekey-disabled profiles which are at risk of PN rollover on high-throughput tunnels. Use 'spi-lookup detail' (order 13) and 'out-sa-hash' (order 12) to confirm the inbound / outbound SAs are armed in the IOS fast-path lookup tables — missing entries here mean SAs were negotiated but never installed. For IPv6 use 'sa ipv6 detailed' (order 21); on Argon the keyword is 'detailed' (not 'detail')

runbook_e9a68081.name: IPsec Tunnel Interface Diagnostics
runbook_e9a68081.description: Focused diagnostic for GRE/IPsec tunnel interface issues including interface state, MTU/fragmentation, keepalives, routing, and tunnel source/destination reachability. Run 'IPsec Common Baseline' parent workflow first.
symptoms: Tunnel interface up but no traffic
SVI-sourced tunnel up but traffic fails
Tunnel works with Loopback source but not SVI source

cli_commands.purpose: Full tunnel interface details — line protocol state, MTU, bandwidth, input/output packets, drops, errors, tunnel source/destination, tunnel mode (GRE/IPsec), and keepalive settings

interpretation_rules.message: Tunnel interface line protocol is down — tunnel cannot forward traffic
interpretation_rules.recommendation: Check tunnel source interface state and tunnel destination reachability via the underlay routing table

interpretation_rules.message: Input errors detected on tunnel interface — possible decapsulation failures
interpretation_rules.recommendation: Check for MTU issues, fragmentation problems, or malformed tunnel packets

interpretation_rules.message: Significant output drops on tunnel interface — possible congestion or encapsulation failures
interpretation_rules.recommendation: Check tunnel bandwidth, QoS policy, and crypto engine processing capacity

cli_commands.purpose: Review tunnel interface configuration — tunnel source, destination, mode (gre ip, ipsec ipv4, etc.), tunnel protection profile, IP address, keepalive, and MTU/MSS

Verify underlay routing to tunnel destination — tunnel endpoint must be reachable via the underlay IGP. Recursive routing through the tunnel itself causes flap. NOTE: For FVRF tunnels use 'show ip route vrf {fvrf} {tunnel_destination}' instead (see Underlay and VRF Diagnostics sub-runbook)

interpretation_rules.message: No route to tunnel destination IP — tunnel cannot establish without underlay reachability
interpretation_rules.recommendation: Add a static or IGP route to the tunnel destination via the physical underlay interface, not via the tunnel itself. If tunnel uses 'tunnel vrf', check routing in the FVRF with the Underlay and VRF Diagnostics sub-runbook

cli_commands.purpose: VRF-aware route check for tunnel destination — required when tunnel has 'tunnel vrf {fvrf}' configured. The underlay routing for the tunnel lives in the FVRF, not the global table. Most DMVPN and multi-VRF IPsec deployments use FVRF

interpretation_rules.field: Route to tunnel destination in FVRF
interpretation_rules.message: No route to tunnel destination in FVRF — IKE cannot reach the peer. Check IGP neighbor state in the FVRF
interpretation_rules.recommendation: Run 'show ospfv3 vrf {fvrf} neighbor' to verify IGP adjacency. If no neighbors, the transit router may have lost its FVRF config. See Underlay and VRF Diagnostics sub-runbook

interpretation_rules.message: FVRF routing table does not exist — VRF may not be defined or 'ip routing' is disabled
interpretation_rules.recommendation: Verify 'ip routing' is enabled globally and VRF is defined with 'show ip vrf'

cli_commands.purpose: Verify CEF forwarding entry for tunnel destination — check that the outgoing interface is a physical/SVI interface and NOT the tunnel interface itself (recursive routing loop). For FVRF tunnels, use 'show ip cef vrf {fvrf} {tunnel_destination}'

Verify basic IP reachability to tunnel destination from the tunnel source IP. Failure indicates underlay path issue, ACL blocking, or firewall filtering UDP 500/4500. For FVRF tunnels use 'ping vrf {fvrf} {tunnel_destination} source {tunnel_source_ip}'

Verify tunnel source interface state. For SVI sources: line protocol requires at least one trunk port carrying that VLAN. For Loopback: should always be up. Known issue (CSCwt84307): SVI-sourced tunnels may have FED race condition during SA rekey causing traffic failure despite tunnel showing UP

interpretation_rules.message: Tunnel source interface is down — tunnel cannot establish or pass traffic
interpretation_rules.recommendation: For SVI: verify VLAN and trunk ports. For physical: check link/transceiver. For Loopback: check VRF assignment

cli_commands.purpose: Check path MTU to tunnel destination — MTU issues cause fragmentation or black-holing of large packets. Tunnel overhead (GRE=24, IPsec=50-73) reduces effective MTU
Check what routes are pointing into the tunnel interface. Verify expected overlay networks are routed via tunnel
Interface switching statistics — hardware vs software switching, packet counts per switching path. Useful for performance analysis

Crypto session state for a specific tunnel interface — confirms IPsec protection is active on this tunnel and shows peer address, IKEv2/IPsec SA status. Quick way to correlate tunnel interface to its crypto session

cli_commands.command: show platform software fed switch active ip route {tunnel_subnet} detail
cli_commands.purpose: FED-level route programming for the tunnel overlay subnet. Verifies the route is installed in hardware with correct next-hop pointing to the tunnel interface IF_ID. Missing or incorrect FED route = traffic not forwarded in hardware

analysis_points: Tunnel interface must be up/up — line protocol down means tunnel source interface is down or destination is unreachable
Tunnel destination must be reachable via underlay routing — NOT via the tunnel itself (recursive routing causes flap)
VRF-AWARE CHECK: If tunnel has 'tunnel vrf {fvrf}', check reachability in the FVRF, not the global table. Use 'show ip route vrf {fvrf} {tunnel_dest}' and 'ping vrf {fvrf}'
CRITICAL: Tunnel mode must match between peers — GRE/IPsec or pure IPsec (tunnel mode ipsec ipv4). Mode mismatch = no tunnel
Tunnel protection profile must reference a valid IPsec profile with correct transform-set and IKEv2 profile

TUNNEL SOURCE TYPES:
  - Loopback: Most reliable, always up unless VRF removed
  - Physical: Depends on link state
  - SVI: Requires VLAN + trunk port; susceptible to FED race condition (CSCwt84307) during SA rekey — tunnel shows UP but L3Tunnel IPSEC parameter update is skipped because SVI resolves after the tunnel. Workaround: shut/no shut the underlay link
Effective MTU = physical MTU - GRE overhead (24) - IPsec overhead (50-73 depending on algorithm). Default tunnel MTU is 1476 for GRE, adjust ip tcp adjust-mss if needed
Keepalive (if configured) sends periodic GRE keepalive packets — failure to receive replies brings line protocol down
Check CEF entry for tunnel destination — should resolve to physical interface with valid next-hop, never to the tunnel interface
Output drops may indicate crypto engine bottleneck or QoS shaping
For all VRF and underlay issues, drill into the 'IPsec Underlay and VRF Diagnostics' sub-runbook for comprehensive checks

runbook_8cfb3161.name: IPsec Platform and Hardware Diagnostics
runbook_8cfb3161.description: Platform-level IPsec diagnostics for Catalyst 9000 series — FED crypto programming, hardware crypto engine status, TCAM utilization for crypto ACLs, and QFP/datapath statistics. Run 'IPsec Common Baseline' parent workflow first.
symptoms: IPsec tunnel up but very slow throughput
IPsec packet drops at platform level
Tunnel interface not registered in FED
IPsec SA not installed in hardware
Doppler ASIC security engine not initialized
SVI-sourced tunnel UP but traffic fails (FED L3Tunnel race)
FED logs show 'skip L3Tunnel IPSEC parameter update'
FED logs show 'vti is not ready' during SA rekey

cli_commands.purpose: Crypto engine summary — engine type (software/hardware), state, and connection count. Verify hardware crypto engine is active and processing connections
Active crypto engine connections — per-SA encryption/decryption byte counters and algorithm used. Confirms crypto engine is actively processing tunnel traffic

interpretation_rules.message: No active encrypt/decrypt connections in crypto engine — IPsec may not be processing traffic
interpretation_rules.recommendation: Verify IPsec SA is established and traffic is being routed through the tunnel

cli_commands.command: show platform software fed switch active ipsec sa
cli_commands.purpose: FED-level IPsec SA programming — shows SA handle, SPI, direction, algorithm, and hardware programming status. Confirms SAs are installed in the hardware forwarding path

cli_commands.command: show platform software fed switch active ipsec counters if-id all
cli_commands.purpose: FED IPsec packet counters for ALL tunnel if-ids — per-interface encrypt/decrypt packet and byte counts, drop counters, and error types. Shows which specific tunnel interface has hardware-level issues

cli_commands.command: show platform software fed switch active ipsec counters if-id {if_id}
cli_commands.purpose: FED IPsec counters for a SPECIFIC tunnel if-id — focused view of encrypt/decrypt/drop counters for one tunnel. Use after identifying the problem tunnel's IF_ID from ifm interfaces tunnel output

interpretation_rules.message: FED IPsec encrypt drops detected for this tunnel — hardware is failing to encrypt outbound packets
interpretation_rules.recommendation: Check SA programming in FED, verify transform-set is hardware-supported, and check for resource exhaustion

interpretation_rules.message: FED IPsec decrypt drops detected for this tunnel — hardware is failing to decrypt inbound packets
interpretation_rules.recommendation: Check for SPI mismatch, SA expiry, or anti-replay failures at hardware level

cli_commands.command: show platform software fed switch active ipsec resources if-id {if_id}
cli_commands.purpose: FED IPsec resource allocation for a specific tunnel — SA handles, hardware object IDs, encryption context, and resource usage. Confirms all IPsec resources are allocated and programmed for this tunnel

cli_commands.command: show platform software fed switch active ifm interfaces tunnel
cli_commands.purpose: FED interface manager view of ALL tunnel interfaces — shows IF_ID, port type, and hardware mapping. Use the IF_ID values from here as input for per-if-id commands. Confirms tunnels are registered in FED

cli_commands.command: show platform software fed switch active ifm if-id {if_id}
cli_commands.purpose: Detailed FED interface manager info for a specific IF_ID — interface state, port type, encap type, hardware handles, and associated IPsec context. Correlates tunnel interface to hardware programming objects

cli_commands.command: show platform software fed switch active ifm if-id all
cli_commands.purpose: Complete FED interface manager dump for all IF_IDs — useful for cross-referencing tunnel IF_IDs with other interface types and verifying no conflicts

cli_commands.command: show platform hardware fed switch active fwd-asic resource tcam utilization
cli_commands.purpose: TCAM utilization — crypto ACL entries consume TCAM space. If the relevant TCAM region is full, new crypto ACL entries cannot be installed and traffic won't match

cli_commands.command: show platform software fed switch active acl info db summary
cli_commands.purpose: ACL database summary in FED — verify crypto ACLs are programmed and have correct entry counts

cli_commands.command: show processes cpu sorted | include Crypto
cli_commands.purpose: Check CPU utilization by crypto-related processes — high crypto process CPU may indicate software fallback instead of hardware acceleration

interpretation_rules.message: Crypto process CPU usage above 10% — possible software crypto fallback instead of hardware acceleration
interpretation_rules.recommendation: Verify hardware crypto engine is active with show crypto engine brief. Software crypto under high load may cause drops

cli_commands.command: show platform software fed switch active punt cause summary | include crypto|ipsec|Crypto|IPsec
cli_commands.purpose: Check if IPsec/crypto packets are being punted to CPU. Control-plane punt for crypto is expected for IKE, but data-plane punt indicates hardware offload issue

cli_commands.command: show platform software fed switch active l3tunnel all
cli_commands.purpose: FED L3Tunnel programming state for ALL tunnels — shows tunnel IF_ID, source/destination IPs, tunnel mode, VRF, and programming status. CRITICAL for SVI-sourced tunnels: if the L3Tunnel entry shows incomplete IPSEC parameters, the FED skipped the IPsec update due to a race condition where the SVI resolved after the tunnel (CSCwt84307). Look for 'skip L3Tunnel IPSEC parameter update' in FED logs correlated with this output

cli_commands.command: show platform software fed switch active l3tunnel {if_id}
cli_commands.purpose: Per-tunnel FED L3Tunnel detail for a specific IF_ID — shows IPsec SA binding, encryption parameters, and whether the VTI is marked as ready. If 'vti is not ready' appears in FED logs for this IF_ID, the L3Tunnel-to-IPsec binding failed. Get IF_ID from 'ifm interfaces tunnel'

cli_commands.command: show logging process fed internal start last boot | include ipsec|crypto|tunnel|l3tunnel|L3Tunnel|vti
cli_commands.purpose:
FED internal logs for IPsec/crypto/tunnel/L3Tunnel — programming errors, SA install failures, VTI readiness issues, or resource exhaustion events. Key patterns:
  'skip L3Tunnel ... IPSEC parameter update' = race condition on SVI-sourced tunnels
  'vti is not ready' = L3Tunnel-to-IPsec binding failure
  'Successfully created' = normal tunnel programming

Comprehensive IPsec platform tech-support bundle — collects all platform-level IPsec state in one command: FED SA table, counters, resources, TCAM entries, ACL programming, and interface mappings. Use for TAC escalation or when multiple platform CLIs are needed at once. WARNING: produces large output

cli_commands.command: show platform hardware fed switch active fwd-asic insight ipsec_security_association
cli_commands.purpose: Silicon-One ASIC-level IPsec SA table — emits two column-tabular rows per device id: one INGRESS row (SPI, ESN, Anti-Replay, Algorithm, Current PN, Decrypt Bytes/Pkts, Decrypt Errors, Auth Errors, H_Value, Key, Key_Extra) and one EGRESS row (SPI, ESN, Algorithm, Next PN, Encrypt Bytes/Pkts, Encrypt Errors, H_Value, Key, Key_Extra). 'Auth Errors' (CSCwu24703) surfaces GCM ICV / HMAC verification failures previously visible only via the SDK debug shell. ESN and Anti-Replay reflect the SDK ESN / anti-replay window state. Essential for confirming SAs are programmed in hardware and traffic is being processed at the ASIC level

interpretation_rules.message: Zero Encrypt Pkts on the EGRESS row — hardware crypto engine is not processing outbound traffic
interpretation_rules.recommendation: Check SA programming and verify platform ipsec is enabled. Compare with show crypto ipsec sa encaps for software-fallback detection

interpretation_rules.message: Zero Decrypt Pkts on the INGRESS row — hardware crypto engine is not processing inbound traffic
interpretation_rules.recommendation: Verify peer is sending encrypted traffic and INGRESS SPI matches show crypto ipsec sa

interpretation_rules.message: Non-zero Auth Errors on INGRESS row — GCM ICV tag / HMAC verification is failing on inbound ESP. Indicates key mismatch, SPI rollover/rekey skew, anti-replay-window violation, or peer sending corrupted/spoofed ESP
interpretation_rules.recommendation: Cross-check SPI and key material with the peer (show crypto ipsec sa peer X.X.X.X detail). If the tunnel is mid-volume-rekey, verify both sides honor the new SA before expiring the old one. Compare with 'show platform hardware fed switch active ipsec counters if-id all' Auth Error Packets

interpretation_rules.message: Non-zero Decrypt Errors on INGRESS row — ASIC rejected inbound ESP for non-auth reasons (length/format/policy)
interpretation_rules.recommendation: Inspect 'show platform hardware fed switch active ipsec counters if-id all' for Late/Invalid SA Packets and check tunnel MTU + DF-bit

interpretation_rules.message: Non-zero Encrypt Errors on EGRESS row — ASIC failed to encrypt outbound packets (usually template/key-context error)
interpretation_rules.recommendation: Re-check SA install via ipsec_session_oid and crypto map binding. Clear crypto sa peer to reprogram

interpretation_rules.message: ESN (Extended Sequence Number) is disabled on the INGRESS SA (CSCwu24703 column from fwd-asic insight). With AES-GCM the 32-bit ESP sequence number wraps after 2^32 packets — at sustained ~1 Gbps line rate the rollover lands in roughly 4-6 hours. Without ESN the wrap causes catastrophic GCM nonce reuse (the same {key, IV} pair encrypts two distinct plaintexts), which makes key recovery theoretically possible and breaks confidentiality of every packet sent under that SA
interpretation_rules.recommendation: Enable ESN on BOTH peers via the IKEv2 proposal / IPsec transform-set ('crypto ipsec security-association replay window-size 64' with 'esn' keyword, or per-profile ESN enable). Cross-check with 'show crypto ipsec sa detail' for the negotiated 'replay detection support' line. ESN is mandatory for GCM at high throughput and required for FIPS / Common Criteria builds. If the peer cannot negotiate ESN, enable kilobyte-volume rekey (~1 GB lifetime) so the SA rotates before PN rollover

interpretation_rules.message: Anti-Replay is disabled on the INGRESS SA (CSCwu24703 column from fwd-asic insight). The receiver will accept replayed ESP packets without checking the sequence number window — an attacker who captures ciphertext can re-inject it and the data-plane will decrypt and forward it as if fresh. Acceptable in some lab / SD-WAN topologies that do their own replay protection at L7; NOT acceptable on a production customer tunnel
interpretation_rules.recommendation: Verify the configuration intent: if anti-replay was intentionally disabled (e.g. asymmetric routing causing legitimate out-of-window drops), document it in the operator runbook. If unintentional, re-enable with 'crypto ipsec security-association replay window-size <N>' (default 64; increase to 128/256/512/1024 for high-jitter paths). Cross-check with 'show crypto ipsec sa detail' for the 'replay detection support' and 'replay window' lines

cli_commands.command: show platform hardware fed switch active fwd-asic insight ipsec_security_engine
cli_commands.purpose: Doppler ASIC security engine connectivity — shows Security Engine IDs, Handler OIDs, System Port GIDs, System Port Cookies (Se1/Se2), and Security Port OIDs. Confirms the crypto security engines are registered and connected to the forwarding ASIC. Empty table indicates hardware crypto engine is not initialized or available

interpretation_rules.message: No security engine entries found — hardware crypto engine may not be initialized
interpretation_rules.recommendation: Verify platform ipsec is enabled and the hardware supports crypto offload. Check for licensing or hardware faults

cli_commands.command: show platform hardware fed switch active fwd-asic insight ipsec_tunnels
cli_commands.purpose: Doppler ASIC IPsec tunnel port table — shows Tunnel GID, Local IP Prefix, Remote IP, Underlay/Overlay VRFs, Ingress/Egress Session OIDs, and Ingress/Egress SA SPIs. Maps the tunnel to its ASIC-level forwarding objects. Verifies tunnel is programmed in hardware with correct local/remote endpoints and associated SA SPIs

interpretation_rules.message: Tunnel has zero Ingress SA SPI at ASIC level — no inbound SA programmed in hardware
interpretation_rules.recommendation: Clear crypto sa and re-establish tunnel. Verify SA is created and programmed into hardware via show crypto ipsec sa

cli_commands.command: show platform hardware fed switch active fwd-asic insight ipsec_session_oid
cli_commands.purpose: Doppler ASIC IPsec session OID table — shows Session OID, Direction (INGRESS/EGRESS), System Port GID, System Port Cookie (Se1/Se2), and associated Security Associations. Links IPsec sessions to security engine ports and SAs at the ASIC level. Cross-reference with ipsec_tunnels() Session OIDs to trace the full hardware forwarding path

cli_commands.command: show platform software ipsec policy statistics detail
cli_commands.purpose: Detailed PAL (Platform Abstraction Layer) policy statistics — same fields as 'policy statistics' (PAL CMD request/reply/error, PAL NOTIFY receive/complete, IPSec PAL DB summary) plus per-database breakdown. Non-zero REPLY_ERR or PROC_ERR columns are the canonical signal that a control-plane SA install was rejected by the platform. Use after a tunnel-up event that did not result in hardware programming

interpretation_rules.message: Non-zero REPLY_ERR in PAL CMD table — at least one platform IPsec command was rejected by the data-plane (typical: SADB_ATTR_UPDATE / ACL_INSERT / SPI_INSERT failure during SA install)
interpretation_rules.recommendation: Cross-check 'show platform software fed switch active ipsec sa' for the SA in question; if absent, the SA was never programmed in hardware and the tunnel is running in software fallback or black-holing

interpretation_rules.message: Non-zero PROC_ERR in PAL NOTIFY table — a data-plane notification (NOTIFY_RP / SA_DEAD / SA_SOFT_LIFE / IDLE_TIMER / DPD_TIMER) failed to be processed by IOSd
interpretation_rules.recommendation: Inspect 'show logging | include CRYPTO|IPSEC' for matching error messages and correlate timestamps with tunnel flap events

cli_commands.purpose: Platform IPsec inventory counters — running totals of IPsec SADB / SA / interface objects allocated and freed by the platform layer. Compares directly against IOSd control-plane counts; a delta means objects leaked or were stale-cleaned

Platform ISAKMP/IKEv2 inventory counters — running totals of ISAKMP/IKE SA objects allocated by the platform layer. Useful when IKE SAs appear UP at IOSd level but data-plane is not seeing the corresponding state

cli_commands.command: show platform software ipsec isakmp-sa all
cli_commands.purpose: All ISAKMP SAs as seen by the platform layer — platform-level mirror of 'show crypto ikev2 sa'. Mismatch between IOSd and platform views indicates a sync problem between the control plane and the data plane (often the trigger for tunnel-up-but-no-traffic). For one SA use 'show platform software ipsec isakmp-sa identifier <id>'

Modular-exponentiation (DH key-derivation) throttle statistics — reports if the IKE crypto worker is throttling key-exchange operations under load. Non-zero throttle counters explain slow IKE_SA_INIT / rekey on busy aggregation hubs (many simultaneous DH computations)

Platform FTM (Forwarding Manager) message statistics for IPsec — counts of programming messages exchanged between IOSd and the FTM/FED layer. Non-incrementing or error-incrementing counters during a tunnel up/down event indicate the SA never reached the data-plane

Recent HAPI (Hardware Abstraction Programming Interface) message trace for IPsec — last N programming messages sent to the FED. Diagnostic only (latest events): pair with 'ftm-msg-stats' for counters and FED logs for deeper context

analysis_points: Crypto engine must show hardware type (not software-only) for hardware-accelerated IPsec on Cat9k platforms
FED IPsec SA output must show SAs installed with valid handles — missing entries mean hardware is not offloading crypto
FED IPsec counters (if-id all) should show incrementing encrypt/decrypt with zero drops — non-zero drops indicate hardware forwarding issues. Drill into specific if-id for problem tunnel
FED IPsec resources (if-id) must show allocated SA handles and encryption context — missing resources mean SA is not programmed

WORKFLOW: Use 'show platform software fed ... ifm interfaces tunnel' to get IF_IDs, then drill into per-if-id commands: ipsec counters if-id, ipsec resources if-id, and ifm if-id for the problem tunnel
TCAM utilization for ACL regions should not exceed 90% — crypto ACLs share TCAM with regular ACLs on Cat9k
CRITICAL: High CPU from Crypto processes with low hardware crypto engine byte counters indicates software fallback — check if transform-set uses algorithms not supported in hardware
FED interface manager must show tunnel interfaces with valid IF_IDs — missing entries indicate FED programming failure
Punt cause summary should show minimal crypto-related punts — only IKE control-plane traffic should be punted, not data-plane
Check FED internal logs for ipsec/crypto errors during SA install or tunnel interface creation
Use 'show tech-support ipsec platform' for TAC escalation — it bundles all platform IPsec state in one shot

DOPPLER ASIC WORKFLOW: Run ipsec_security_engine() first to verify crypto engines are connected, then ipsec_tunnels() to verify tunnel programming, then ipsec_security_association() to verify SAs are installed with incrementing encrypt/decrypt counters, and finally ipsec_session_oid() to trace session-to-SA-to-engine mapping

CRITICAL: If ipsec_security_association() shows zero encrypt/decrypt packets but show crypto ipsec sa shows non-zero encaps/decaps, traffic is being processed in software (CPU) not hardware — check ipsec_security_engine() for engine availability
Cross-reference SPIs: show crypto ipsec sa SPI values must match ipsec_security_association() SPI and ipsec_tunnels() Ingress/Egress SA SPI values. Mismatches indicate stale hardware programming

L3TUNNEL WORKFLOW: Run 'show platform software fed ... l3tunnel all' to see FED L3Tunnel programming. For SVI-sourced tunnels, check if the IPsec parameters are fully populated. If missing, the FED hit a race condition (CSCwt84307) where the L3Tunnel update was skipped because the SVI had not yet resolved when the tunnel was programmed

SVI RACE CONDITION (CSCwt84307): In the FED programming sequence, tunnel creation triggers an IPsec parameter update via la_ipsec_npe2_l3tunnel_api. If the tunnel source is an SVI and the SVI interface has not yet been fully programmed (common during rekey or reboot), the update is skipped with 'vti is not ready'. The tunnel shows UP at the IOS level but data-plane traffic fails. Workaround: shut/no shut the underlay link to re-trigger FED programming. Check FED logs for 'skip L3Tunnel' messages

ASIC SA TABLE COLUMNS (CSCwu24703 / fwd-asic insight): the new tabular output prints one INGRESS row and one EGRESS row per device id. Ingress row exposes 'Auth Errors' (GCM ICV / HMAC verification failures) and 'Decrypt Errors' (length / format / policy drops) — previously these counts were only visible from the SDK debug shell, with insight reporting Errors:0. Egress row exposes 'Encrypt Errors'. ESN and Anti-Replay reflect esn-enable and anti-replay-en from the SDK SA. Use this view FIRST when the operator complains of high RX_METER drops, tunnel up but no traffic, or volume-based-rekey corruption — non-zero Auth Errors on INGRESS is the smoking gun for ICV/HMAC key skew between peers

ARGON (C9350) PAL/HAPI INVENTORY: orders 21-27 add platform-layer drill-downs verified on RAITT-41. 'policy statistics detail' (order 21) is the canonical signal for SA-install rejection — non-zero REPLY_ERR / PROC_ERR columns mean a control-plane SA was rejected by the data-plane. 'isakmp-sa all' (order 24) is the platform mirror of 'show crypto ikev2 sa'; mismatch between the two views indicates a control-plane / data-plane sync issue, the classic cause of tunnel-up-but-no-traffic. 'modexp-throttle-stats' (order 25) explains slow IKE_SA_INIT / rekey on busy aggregation hubs (DH key-derivation throttled). 'ftm-msg-stats' and 'hapi-msg-trace' (orders 26-27) confirm whether SA programming messages reached FTM/FED. NOTE: Doppler-only commands ('pal-cmd-list', 'fp active inventory', 'mode', 'transform-proposal') are NOT registered on Argon — use the above C9350-verified set instead

runbook_b5d018b1.name: IPsec Stack Diagnostics
runbook_b5d018b1.description: Stack-specific IPsec diagnostics for C9350/C9500 stacked deployments. IMPORTANT: First run 'show switch' to detect stack members and their roles (Active/Standby/Member). Then repeat all FED and fwd-asic insight commands for EACH switch member number. This workflow contains per-member variants using '{switch_number}' placeholder — replace with the actual switch number (1, 2, 3, ...) from 'show switch' output. For standalone switches (single member), use Sub-workflow 4 with 'switch active' instead.
symptoms: IPsec tunnel works on standalone but fails in stack
Traffic drops when ingressing on non-active stack member
IPsec SA not programmed on all stack members
Hardware crypto offload only on active member
Stack member failover causes IPsec tunnel flap
Inconsistent encrypt/decrypt counters across stack members
FED IPsec programming error on specific stack member
Stack ring congestion affecting crypto traffic

cli_commands.purpose: Detect stack topology — lists all stack members, their roles (Active/Standby/Member), MAC addresses, priority, and hardware version. If only one member exists, the switch is standalone. For stacks, note each member number and repeat subsequent FED commands per member
Stack port link status and cable length — verifies physical stack ring connectivity. Down stack ports can cause member isolation and inconsistent FED programming of IPsec SAs across members

cli_commands.command: show platform hardware fed switch {switch_number} fwd-asic insight ipsec_security_engine
cli_commands.purpose: Per-member Doppler ASIC security engine connectivity — replace {switch_number} with each stack member number. Confirms crypto engines are registered on EACH member's forwarding ASIC. All members must have valid engine entries for hardware crypto acceleration to work

cli_commands.command: show platform hardware fed switch {switch_number} fwd-asic insight ipsec_security_association
cli_commands.purpose: Per-member Doppler ASIC SA table — replace {switch_number} with each stack member number. Shows SPI, direction, algorithm, encrypt/decrypt counters per member. In a stack, only the member whose physical port is the tunnel source will have active SA entries; other members forward to the active crypto member via the stack ring

cli_commands.command: show platform hardware fed switch {switch_number} fwd-asic insight ipsec_tunnels
cli_commands.purpose: Per-member Doppler ASIC tunnel port table — replace {switch_number} with each stack member number. Verifies tunnel is programmed on the correct member with matching local/remote IPs and SA SPIs

cli_commands.command: show platform hardware fed switch {switch_number} fwd-asic insight ipsec_session_oid
cli_commands.purpose: Per-member Doppler ASIC session OID table — replace {switch_number} with each stack member number. Cross-reference with ipsec_tunnels() Session OIDs to verify session-to-SA-to-engine mapping per member

cli_commands.command: show platform hardware fed switch {switch_number} ipsec sa
cli_commands.purpose: Per-member FED IPsec SA table — replace {switch_number} with each stack member number. Shows SA handles, SPI, interface, and status. Complements the fwd-asic insight SA view with FED-level SA programming details

cli_commands.command: show platform hardware fed switch {switch_number} ipsec counters if-id all
cli_commands.purpose: Per-member FED IPsec counters for all tunnel if-ids — replace {switch_number} with each stack member number. Shows encrypt/decrypt packet and byte counts, drops, and error statistics per member. Compare across members to verify traffic is being processed on the expected member

cli_commands.command: show platform software fed switch {switch_number} ifm interfaces tunnel
cli_commands.purpose: Per-member FED interface manager tunnel state — replace {switch_number} with each stack member number. Shows IF_IDs for tunnel interfaces on each member. All stack members should have the tunnel registered; missing entries indicate FED programming inconsistency

cli_commands.command: show platform hardware fed switch {switch_number} ipsec resources if-id {if_id}
cli_commands.purpose: Per-member FED IPsec resources for a specific tunnel if-id — replace {switch_number} and {if_id}. Shows allocated SA handles, encryption contexts, and TCAM entries per member for the problem tunnel

analysis_points: FIRST: Run 'show switch' — if only 1 member is listed, the switch is standalone; use Sub-workflow 4 instead of this one
STACK WORKFLOW: For each member N in 'show switch', repeat commands 3-10 by replacing {switch_number} with N (1, 2, 3, ...)
STACK RING: 'show switch stack-ports' must show all links Up — a broken stack ring can cause FED programming inconsistencies where IPsec SAs are installed on some members but not others

SA DISTRIBUTION: In a stack, IPsec SAs are programmed on the active member that owns the tunnel source physical port. Traffic arriving on other members is forwarded via the stack ring to the crypto member — verify stack ring bandwidth is sufficient

COUNTER COMPARISON: Compare ipsec_security_association() encrypt/decrypt counters across members — only the crypto-owner member should show incrementing counters; other members should show zero unless load-balancing is configured
ENGINE CHECK: ipsec_security_engine() should show valid crypto engines on ALL members — empty tables on any member indicate that member cannot perform hardware crypto if traffic arrives on its ports
FAILOVER: After stack master switchover, verify IPsec SAs are reprogrammed on the new active member by checking ipsec_security_association() on both old and new active

IF_ID CONSISTENCY: The tunnel IF_ID from 'ifm interfaces tunnel' should be the same across all members — different IF_IDs indicate FED programming inconsistency that requires clearing crypto SAs and bouncing the tunnel interface

runbook_5633fa61.name: IPsec Configuration Cleanup
runbook_5633fa61.description: Step-by-step IPsec configuration removal runbook for Catalyst 9000 series. Follows the mandatory dependency order: tunnel protection and tunnel interfaces must be removed first, then IPsec profiles and transform-sets, then IKEv2 profiles/keyrings/policies/proposals. Reversing this order causes CLI errors because each layer references the one below it. After config removal, clear residual SAs and verify cleanup is complete. Run 'show running-config | section crypto' BEFORE starting to inventory all IPsec objects that need removal.
symptoms: Need to remove all IPsec configuration
Cleaning up IPsec config after testing
Stale crypto config causing tunnel failures
Cannot modify IPsec profile — in use error
Cannot remove keyring — referenced by profile
Orphaned crypto objects after partial cleanup
Need to start IPsec configuration from scratch
Clearing IPsec config for lab reset

cli_commands.purpose: PREREQUISITE: Inventory all crypto objects before removal. Note down every proposal, policy, keyring, IKEv2 profile, transform-set, IPsec profile, and tunnel interface that references them. This output drives the removal sequence

cli_commands.command: show ip interface brief | include Tunnel
cli_commands.purpose: List all tunnel interfaces and their current state. Each tunnel with 'tunnel protection ipsec profile' must be cleaned up in Phase 1
Quick view of active crypto sessions before teardown. Note the peer IPs and session states — active sessions will be disrupted during cleanup

STEP 1 — Remove tunnel protection FIRST, then shut down the tunnel interface. The 'no tunnel protection' detaches the IPsec profile from the tunnel, which triggers SA teardown for this tunnel. Repeat for EACH tunnel interface. MUST be done before removing the IPsec profile itself
STEP 2 — Delete the tunnel interface entirely after protection is removed and it is shut down. Repeat for EACH tunnel interface. This releases the IF_ID and FED resources associated with the tunnel

STEP 3 — Remove the IPsec profile. This can only succeed after ALL tunnel interfaces referencing this profile have had their 'tunnel protection' removed. The IPsec profile binds a transform-set and optionally an IKEv2 profile

STEP 4 — Remove the IPsec transform-set. This can only succeed after ALL IPsec profiles referencing this transform-set have been removed. The transform-set defines ESP encryption and integrity algorithms
STEP 5 — Remove the IKEv2 profile. Must be done AFTER the IPsec profile that references it is removed. The IKEv2 profile binds match criteria (address/FVRF), authentication method, and the keyring
STEP 6 — Remove the IKEv2 keyring. Must be done AFTER the IKEv2 profile that references it is removed. The keyring holds pre-shared keys for peer authentication
STEP 7 — Remove the IKEv2 policy. The policy binds an IKEv2 proposal with a match priority. Safe to remove after the IKEv2 profile is gone since no sessions can negotiate using this policy
STEP 8 — Remove the IKEv2 proposal. Must be done AFTER the IKEv2 policy that references it is removed. The proposal defines encryption, integrity, PRF, and DH group algorithms for Phase 1 negotiation
STEP 9 — Clear all residual IPsec Security Associations. After config removal, stale SAs may remain in memory. This forces immediate cleanup of all IPsec SA state
STEP 10 — Clear all residual IKEv2 Security Associations. Removes any lingering IKEv2 SA entries that survived the config removal. Ensures no orphaned IKE sessions remain
VERIFY: Confirm no crypto configuration remains. Output should be empty or contain only unrelated crypto config (e.g., SSH keys). Any leftover IPsec/IKEv2 objects indicate incomplete cleanup

interpretation_rules.message: Residual IPsec/IKEv2 configuration found after cleanup — some objects were not removed
interpretation_rules.recommendation: Review the remaining config and repeat the removal steps for any leftover objects. Check for cross-references that prevented deletion

cli_commands.purpose: VERIFY: Confirm no crypto sessions remain active. Output should show no sessions or return empty. Any remaining sessions indicate SA cleanup was incomplete
VERIFY: SA count should show zero active, zero rekeying. Non-zero counts after cleanup indicate orphaned SAs — run 'clear crypto sa' again if needed

interpretation_rules.message: Active IPsec SAs still present after cleanup
interpretation_rules.recommendation: Run 'clear crypto sa' to force removal of residual SAs

cli_commands.command: show ip interface brief | include Tunnel
cli_commands.purpose: VERIFY: Confirm all tunnel interfaces have been removed. No Tunnel interfaces should appear in the output unless they are non-IPsec tunnels (e.g., GRE-only)

cli_commands.command: show platform software fed switch active ipsec sa
cli_commands.purpose: VERIFY: Confirm FED has no residual IPsec SA programming. Empty output confirms hardware-level cleanup is complete. Leftover FED entries may cause issues when re-configuring IPsec tunnels

analysis_points: DEPENDENCY ORDER IS MANDATORY: tunnel interface -> IPsec profile -> transform-set -> IKEv2 profile -> keyring -> policy -> proposal. Skipping steps or reversing order causes 'object in use' errors
ALWAYS inventory with 'show run | section crypto' before starting — you need exact object names for each removal command
TUNNEL PROTECTION FIRST: The 'no tunnel protection' command MUST precede 'no interface Tunnel' — removing the interface while protection is active can leave orphaned SA state
REPEAT PER TUNNEL: If multiple tunnels exist, remove tunnel protection and delete each tunnel interface individually in Phase 1 before moving to Phase 2
IPSEC PROFILE BEFORE TRANSFORM-SET: The IPsec profile references the transform-set — removing the transform-set first will fail if the profile still exists
IKEV2 PROFILE BEFORE KEYRING: The IKEv2 profile references the keyring — removing the keyring first will fail if the profile still references it
CLEAR SAs AFTER CONFIG REMOVAL: Even after removing all config, residual SAs may linger in memory. 'clear crypto sa' and 'clear crypto ikev2 sa' ensure complete state cleanup
VERIFY FED CLEANUP: After all IOS-level cleanup, check FED with 'show platform software fed ... ipsec sa' — stale FED entries can cause programming failures when reconfiguring IPsec
CAUTION: This runbook is DESTRUCTIVE — all IPsec tunnels will go down permanently. Ensure you have the config saved or documented before proceeding if you plan to reconfigure later

runbook_2e786a3e.name: IPsec Underlay and VRF Diagnostics
runbook_2e786a3e.description: Focused diagnostic for underlay reachability and VRF health in FVRF/IVRF IPsec deployments. Covers VRF existence, interface assignments, IGP neighbor state in the FVRF, tunnel source interface validation (Loopback vs SVI vs physical), and transit path reachability. CRITICAL: When tunnels show DOWN-NEGOTIATING or crypto sessions fail to establish, run this sub-runbook BEFORE the IKEv2/IPsec SA sub-runbooks — the most common cause of DOWN-NEGOTIATING is underlay unreachability, not crypto misconfiguration. Run 'IPsec Common Baseline' parent runbook first.
symptoms: FVRF routing table empty or missing
OSPF neighbor not forming in FVRF
No OSPF routes in FVRF after device reload
IPsec tunnels down after config restore
Tunnel works with Loopback source but fails with SVI source
All tunnels fail simultaneously — underlay issue

cli_commands.purpose: List all VRFs with their Route Distinguisher and assigned interfaces. Verify FVRF (e.g., WAN-VRF) and IVRF (e.g., client1-vrf) exist with correct RDs. CRITICAL: If the FVRF is missing or has no interfaces, no tunnel can establish — the underlay routing context does not exist

interpretation_rules.message: FVRF has no interfaces assigned — underlay routing cannot function without interfaces in the VRF
interpretation_rules.recommendation: Check if device rebooted and lost config. Verify interface VRF membership with 'show run interface' for each underlay interface

cli_commands.purpose: Detailed per-VRF interface list with IP addresses and protocol status. Verify underlay interfaces (physical, SVI, Loopback) are in the correct FVRF with valid IPs. Also verify tunnel overlay interfaces are in the correct IVRF

Full routing table for the FVRF. Verify underlay routes exist — connected subnets for physical/SVI interfaces, OSPF/EIGRP learned routes for remote tunnel endpoints, and Loopback host routes. An empty table means the underlay is completely broken

interpretation_rules.message: FVRF routing table does not exist — VRF may not be defined or ip routing is not enabled
interpretation_rules.recommendation: Verify VRF definition exists with 'show run | section vrf definition'. Ensure 'ip routing' and 'ipv6 unicast-routing' are enabled globally

interpretation_rules.pattern: Gateway of last resort is not set\s*$
interpretation_rules.message: FVRF routing table is empty — no connected, static, or IGP routes exist
interpretation_rules.recommendation: Check interface VRF membership and interface status. Verify IGP (OSPF/EIGRP) is configured for the FVRF address-family

cli_commands.purpose: Check specific route to tunnel destination IP in FVRF. This is the underlay route the tunnel uses to reach the remote peer. Must exist and resolve via a physical/SVI interface, NOT via the tunnel itself (recursive routing)

interpretation_rules.field: Route to tunnel destination in FVRF
interpretation_rules.message: No route to tunnel destination in FVRF — tunnel cannot establish IKE session without underlay reachability
interpretation_rules.recommendation: Check IGP neighbor state in the FVRF. Verify transit router is advertising the tunnel destination prefix. Use 'show ospfv3 vrf {fvrf} neighbor' to check IGP adjacency

cli_commands.purpose: CEF forwarding entry for tunnel destination in FVRF. Verify next-hop resolves to a physical or SVI interface within the FVRF. A 'no route' or 'drop' adjacency means IKE packets to the peer will be discarded

ICMP reachability test to tunnel destination from the tunnel source IP within the FVRF. This validates the complete underlay path including routing, ARP/ND, and any ACLs/firewalls. Failure here means IKE UDP 500/4500 will also fail

OSPFv3 neighbor state in the FVRF. Verify adjacency with transit/PE routers is FULL. No neighbors = no learned routes = tunnel destinations unreachable. Common cause: transit router lost its FVRF interface config after reboot

interpretation_rules.message: OSPFv3 does not recognize the FVRF — OSPFv3 is not configured for this VRF address-family
interpretation_rules.recommendation: Verify 'router ospfv3' has the correct 'address-family ipv4/ipv6 unicast vrf {fvrf}' configuration. Ensure interfaces in the FVRF have 'ospfv3 {pid} ipv4/ipv6 area' commands

cli_commands.purpose: OSPF-learned routes in the FVRF. These should include remote Loopback IPs (tunnel destinations) and transit subnets. If empty but neighbors are FULL, check route redistribution or area configuration

Verify tunnel source interface is up/up with correct IP. The source can be a Loopback, SVI (Vlan), or physical interface. CRITICAL for SVI sources: line protocol must be up, which requires at least one trunk port carrying that VLAN to be up

interpretation_rules.message: Tunnel source interface line protocol is down — tunnel cannot form when its source is down
interpretation_rules.recommendation: For SVI: check VLAN exists and at least one trunk port carries it. For physical: check cable/transceiver. For Loopback: should always be up unless VRF is misconfigured

cli_commands.purpose: SVI-specific: Verify VLAN exists and has active trunk ports. An SVI-sourced tunnel requires the underlying VLAN to be active with at least one trunk port up. Skip for Loopback or physical tunnel sources
SVI-specific: Verify trunk ports are carrying the VLAN used as tunnel source. If the VLAN is not allowed on any trunk, the SVI will be down/down. Skip for Loopback or physical tunnel sources
ARP table in the FVRF — verify next-hop MACs are learned for transit interfaces. Incomplete ARP entries indicate Layer 2 connectivity issues on the underlay

Trace underlay path to tunnel destination in FVRF. Identifies which hop in the transit path is failing. Useful when ping fails but the local interface is up — the problem is likely on a transit router

analysis_points: TRIAGE ORDER: When ALL tunnels are DOWN-NEGOTIATING, the problem is almost always underlay reachability, NOT crypto config. Check this sub-runbook BEFORE IKEv2 or IPsec SA sub-runbooks
VRF EXISTENCE: 'show ip vrf' must show the FVRF with interfaces assigned. An FVRF with no interfaces means the underlay routing context is broken — no IGP can run, no tunnels can establish
ROUTING TABLE: 'show ip route vrf {fvrf}' must have routes to all tunnel destination IPs. These are typically learned via OSPF/EIGRP from transit routers. Empty table = no reachability

IGP NEIGHBOR: Check OSPF/EIGRP neighbor state in the FVRF. If the neighbor to the transit router is not FULL, no remote routes will be learned. Common cause: transit router rebooted and lost its FVRF interface config

TUNNEL SOURCE TYPES have different failure modes:
  - Loopback: Most reliable — always up unless VRF is removed
  - Physical interface: Depends on cable/link state
  - SVI (Vlan interface): Requires VLAN + trunk port to be up; known FED race condition (CSCwt84307) where SVI-sourced tunnels may show UP but fail to pass traffic after SA rekey — the FED L3Tunnel update is skipped because the SVI resolves after the tunnel in the programming sequence. Workaround: link flap

SVI SOURCE VALIDATION: When tunnel source is an SVI, verify:
  1. VLAN exists ('show vlan id {id}')
  2. At least one trunk port carries it ('show interfaces trunk')
  3. SVI is up/up ('show interfaces Vlan{id}')
  4. SVI has correct IP and VRF assignment

TRANSIT PATH: If local interfaces are up and OSPF is configured but no neighbors form, the problem is on the transit router. Use traceroute to identify which hop fails. Common issue: transit router rebooted to default config, losing all VRF and interface configuration

CONFIG PERSISTENCE: After restoring device config, always verify 'ip routing' and 'ipv6 unicast-routing' are enabled globally. Without these, VRF routing tables will not populate even if interfaces and OSPF are configured correctly

SIMULTANEOUS FAILURE: When all tunnels go DOWN-NEGOTIATING at the same time, the root cause is shared infrastructure — underlay interface down, OSPF process crash, VRF deletion, or transit router failure. Individual tunnel failures point to per-tunnel crypto issues instead

runbook_3eeba083.name: NetFlow Common Baseline
runbook_3eeba083.description: Common baseline NetFlow / Flexible NetFlow (FNF) troubleshooting workflow. Run these CLIs first for any NetFlow issue to verify flow record definition, flow monitor state, exporter reachability, and cache population, then drill into a specific sub-workflow based on the symptom. Sub-workflows: NetFlow Configuration and Setup Validation, Flow Record and Monitor Diagnostics, Exporter and Template Diagnostics, Cache and Datapath Diagnostics, Platform FED NetFlow Diagnostics, IPFIX Exporter Diagnostics.
symptoms: Cache timeout too high or too low

cli_commands.purpose: List all configured flow records and their match/collect fields. Verify that the expected key fields (source/dest IP, protocol, source/dest port, direction) are present
Display all flow monitors showing associated record, exporter, cache type, and timeout values. Verify the monitor references the correct record and exporter

interpretation_rules.message: Flow monitor is not in 'allocated' state — monitor may not be actively collecting flows
interpretation_rules.recommendation: Verify the monitor is applied to an interface with 'ip flow monitor <name> input/output'

cli_commands.purpose: Extended view of all monitors including cache details, active/inactive timeouts, and current entry counts
Display all configured exporters with destination IP, source IP, transport protocol/port, export-protocol (NetFlow v9 / IPFIX), DSCP, and TTL settings
Export packet counters: sent, failed, and dropped. Non-zero failures or drops indicate collector unreachable, MTU issues, or export queue overflow

interpretation_rules.message: Flow exporter has send failures — flow data is NOT reaching the collector
interpretation_rules.recommendation: Check collector reachability (ping/traceroute to destination IP), verify source interface is up, and confirm no ACL blocking UDP export port

cli_commands.command: show flow monitor cache format table
cli_commands.purpose: Display the flow cache in table format showing all active flow entries with their key fields and counters. If the cache is empty despite traffic flowing, the monitor may not be applied to the correct interface or direction

interpretation_rules.message: Flow cache is empty — no flows are being recorded
interpretation_rules.recommendation: Verify the flow monitor is applied to the correct interface and direction (input/output). Send test traffic and re-check

cli_commands.command: show flow monitor cache format record | include Current entries
cli_commands.purpose: Quick count of current cache entries without printing all flow data. Useful on high-traffic devices to avoid large output
Flow monitor statistics: total flows seen, active/inactive timeout expirations, and export stats per monitor. High timeout expiration counts are normal for active monitors

Review the full flow configuration: flow records (match/collect fields), flow exporters (destination, transport, DSCP, TTL), flow monitors (record, exporter, cache settings), and interface application

analysis_points: Flow record must have the required match fields (src/dst IP, protocol, src/dst port) for the use case
Flow monitor must reference a valid record and exporter, and be applied to the correct interface with 'ip flow monitor <name> input/output'
Flow exporter must have reachable destination IP, correct transport port (default 2055 or 9996), and a valid source interface
Flow cache entries should be non-zero when traffic is flowing through the monitored interface
Exporter statistics should show sent packets with zero failures — non-zero failures indicate collector reachability or path issues
After running common CLIs, select a sub-workflow based on the specific symptom

runbook_6bb44396.name: NetFlow Configuration and Setup Validation
runbook_6bb44396.description: Verify end-to-end NetFlow / FNF configuration and diagnose setup failures: flow record (match/collect fields), flow exporter (destination, protocol, port), flow monitor (record, exporter, cache size, timeouts, cache type), optional sampler, and interface application. Use when NetFlow is not working due to misconfiguration. IMPORTANT: Configuration must be created in order — record first, then exporter, then monitor, then sampler, then interface application. Run 'NetFlow Common Baseline' parent workflow first.
symptoms: Flow record missing match or collect fields
Flow monitor not referencing record or exporter
Cannot modify flow record while attached
Cache type incorrect for use case
Sampler rate too aggressive or too conservative
NetFlow not collecting after config change

cli_commands.purpose: List all flow records — verify the match fields define the flow key (typically: ipv4 protocol, ipv4 source address, ipv4 destination address, transport source-port, transport destination-port, flow direction) and the collect fields capture the required counters (counter bytes long, counter packets long)

Detail for a specific flow record — verify all match fields for the use case. Standard IP NetFlow record: match ipv4 protocol, match ipv4 source address, match ipv4 destination address, match transport destination-port, match flow direction, collect counter bytes long, collect counter packets long

Verify exporter configuration: destination IP (collector address), transport protocol and port (e.g., transport udp 2005), and export-protocol (ipfix or netflow-v9). Example config: destination 10.3.0.2, transport udp 2005, export-protocol ipfix

Verify monitor configuration: associated record and exporter, cache entries (e.g., 10000 or 65536), cache timeout inactive (e.g., 1 second for fast aging), cache timeout active (e.g., 90 seconds for frequent export of long-lived flows), and cache type (normal / immediate / permanent). Cache type 'normal' ages flows on both active and inactive timeouts

interpretation_rules.message: Cache type is not 'normal' — verify this is intentional for the use case
interpretation_rules.recommendation: 'normal' ages flows on active/inactive timeouts and TCP FIN/RST. 'immediate' exports every packet as a flow (high volume). 'permanent' never ages flows (fixed set of entries)

cli_commands.purpose: List all configured samplers — shows sampler name, mode (random or deterministic), and sampling rate (e.g., 'mode random 1 out-of 10' samples 1 in every 10 packets). Sampling reduces CPU and cache load but provides statistical rather than exact flow data

Detail for a specific sampler — verify the sampling mode and rate. 'mode random 1 out-of 10' means 10% of packets are sampled. Higher rates (1 out-of 100, 1 out-of 1000) reduce load further but decrease accuracy

Running configuration of all flow records — verify match fields (ipv4 protocol, ipv4 source/destination address, transport destination-port, flow direction) and collect fields (counter bytes long, counter packets long). Missing match fields mean those dimensions are not used as cache keys; missing collect fields mean those counters are not tracked
Running configuration of all flow exporters — verify destination (collector IP), transport udp <port>, export-protocol (ipfix or netflow-v9), dscp, and ttl

Running configuration of all flow monitors — verify record and exporter references, cache entries count, cache timeout active/inactive values, and cache type. The monitor binds the record to the exporter with cache parameters
Running configuration of all samplers — verify sampler name and 'mode random 1 out-of <N>' rate. Sampler is optional; if not configured, all packets are monitored

cli_commands.command: show running-config | include ip flow monitor
cli_commands.purpose: All interface-level flow monitor applications — verify each target interface has 'ip flow monitor <name> [sampler <sampler_name>] input' and/or 'output'. With sampler: 'ip flow monitor FM sampler FS input'. Without sampler: 'ip flow monitor FM input'

interpretation_rules.message: No 'ip flow monitor' applied to any interface — NetFlow will not collect any data
interpretation_rules.recommendation: Apply under the interface: 'ip flow monitor <monitor> input' (no sampler) or 'ip flow monitor <monitor> sampler <sampler> input' (with sampler)

analysis_points: CONFIGURATION ORDER: Create flow record first, then flow exporter, then flow monitor (referencing both), then optional sampler, then apply to the interface. This order is mandatory

MODIFICATION CONSTRAINT: While a flow monitor is attached to an interface, changes to the record, exporter, monitor, and sampler are NOT allowed. Remove 'ip flow monitor <name> input/output' from the interface first, make changes, then re-apply

Standard IP NetFlow flow record should include: match ipv4 protocol, match ipv4 source address, match ipv4 destination address, match transport destination-port, match flow direction, collect counter bytes long, collect counter packets long
Flow exporter must specify: destination <collector_ip>, transport udp <port>, and export-protocol ipfix (or netflow-v9)
Flow monitor binds record + exporter and defines cache behaviour: cache entries (10000-65536 typical), cache timeout inactive (1-15 seconds), cache timeout active (30-1800 seconds), cache type normal
Cache timeout inactive = 1 second is very aggressive — good for catching short-lived flows fast but generates high export volume. Default is 15 seconds
Cache timeout active = 90 seconds exports long-lived flows every 90s instead of the default 1800s (30 min) — gives better time granularity at the collector
Sampler is optional. 'mode random 1 out-of 10' samples 10% of packets, reducing CPU/cache load. Without a sampler, 100% of packets on the monitored interface are tracked
Interface application syntax: 'ip flow monitor FM sampler FS input' (with sampler) or 'ip flow monitor FM input' (without sampler)

runbook_27d72a71.name: Flow Record and Monitor Diagnostics
runbook_27d72a71.description: Focused diagnostic for flow record definition and flow monitor configuration issues. Verifies match/collect fields are correct, monitor references the right record/exporter, cache type and timeout tuning, and interface application. Run 'NetFlow Common Baseline' parent workflow first.
symptoms: Flow monitor not referencing correct record
Cache timeout too aggressive or too slow
Flow cache near full or evicting entries

cli_commands.purpose: List all flow records — verify match fields include the required tuple (ipv4 src/dst address, protocol, transport src/dst port). Missing fields mean those dimensions are not tracked in the cache
Detail for a specific flow record — shows all match and collect fields. Verify 'match flow direction' is present if you need to distinguish ingress vs egress
Specific monitor detail — shows associated record, exporter, cache type (normal/immediate/permanent), and timeout values. Active timeout default is 1800s (30 min); inactive timeout default is 15s

interpretation_rules.message: Active timeout is above default 1800s — long-lived flows will be exported less frequently
interpretation_rules.recommendation: Consider lowering active timeout for more granular time-series data at the collector

interpretation_rules.message: Inactive timeout is above 60s — idle flows will consume cache entries longer than necessary
interpretation_rules.recommendation: Default inactive timeout is 15s. Higher values use more cache memory; lower values free entries faster but generate more exports

cli_commands.purpose: All monitors with cache size, current entries, and high watermark. Compare current entries against cache size to detect if the cache is near full (causing evictions)

Running configuration of all flow records — verify match and collect statements match the intended monitoring use case. Common fields: match ipv4 source/destination address, match ipv4 protocol, match transport source/destination-port, match flow direction
Running configuration of all flow monitors — verify record association, exporter binding, and cache timeout settings

cli_commands.command: show running-config | include ip flow monitor
cli_commands.purpose: Show all interface-level flow monitor applications. Verify the monitor is applied to the correct interfaces with the correct direction (input / output / both)

interpretation_rules.message: No 'ip flow monitor' applied to any interface — NetFlow will not collect any data
interpretation_rules.recommendation: Apply the flow monitor under the interface: 'ip flow monitor <name> input' and/or 'ip flow monitor <name> output'

analysis_points: The flow record must include all key match fields required by the use case (5-tuple, direction, ToS, TTL, etc.)
Active timeout controls how often long-lived flows are exported — default 1800s (30 min). Lower values give better time granularity but more export volume
Inactive timeout controls how quickly idle flows are aged out — default 15s. Too high wastes cache; too low generates excess exports
Cache size determines maximum concurrent flows tracked. If current entries approach cache size, flows are prematurely evicted and data is lost
The flow monitor must be applied to the interface under 'ip flow monitor <name> input/output' — configuring the monitor alone is not sufficient

runbook_6a9f93c7.name: Exporter and Template Diagnostics
runbook_6a9f93c7.description: Focused diagnostic for flow exporter configuration, template generation, and delivery issues. Covers destination/source addressing, transport settings, IPFIX vs NetFlow v9, DSCP/TTL, template interval, and collector reachability. Run 'NetFlow Common Baseline' parent workflow first.
symptoms: Exporter not sending data to collector

cli_commands.purpose: Display exporter configuration: destination IP, source IP, transport protocol (UDP), destination port (default 2055 for NetFlow v9, 4739 for IPFIX), DSCP, TTL, and export protocol version
Specific exporter detail — verify destination IP matches the collector address, source IP is routable from the device, and the transport port matches the collector's listening port
Exporter packet counters: total sent, total failed, client send errors. Non-zero failures indicate the export path is broken (unreachable collector, ACL blocking, MTU issue)

interpretation_rules.message: Zero records exported — exporter has never successfully sent flow data
interpretation_rules.recommendation: Verify the monitor has cache entries to export. Check exporter destination reachability and UDP port accessibility

cli_commands.purpose: Display template definitions sent to the collector. NetFlow v9 and IPFIX require template records to be sent before data records — if templates are not being sent, the collector cannot decode the data

cli_commands.command: show flow exporter option application engines
cli_commands.purpose: Show NBAR/AVC application engine option templates — relevant when using application-aware flow records with NBAR application fields
Running configuration of all exporters — verify destination, source, transport udp port, dscp, ttl, export-protocol, and template data timeout settings

Display the exporter application table — shows the mapping of application IDs to names used in flow records when NBAR/AVC application fields are included. Required when the collector needs to decode application names from numeric IDs in the exported data

Display IPFIX export Information Element (IE) IDs — shows the mapping of field names to IPFIX IE numbers used in templates. Essential for verifying the collector can interpret the exported fields correctly
Verify Layer 3 reachability from the exporter source IP to the collector destination IP. Failure indicates routing or firewall issues preventing export delivery

analysis_points: Exporter destination IP must match the collector's listening address, and the transport port must match the collector's configured port
Source IP should be a reachable routed address (often a Loopback) that the collector expects to receive from
Export protocol must match what the collector supports — NetFlow v9 uses template IDs, IPFIX (v10) uses Information Elements
Templates are sent periodically (default 600s). If the collector restarts, it may not have templates until the next template refresh — reduce template timeout for faster recovery
DSCP and TTL settings affect how export packets traverse the network — ensure DSCP is not deprioritized and TTL is sufficient to reach the collector
Non-zero 'Client send statistics' failures in exporter statistics are the primary indicator of export delivery problems

runbook_0b74b55f.name: Cache and Datapath Diagnostics
runbook_0b74b55f.description: Diagnose flow cache population, aging, and datapath failures. Use when NetFlow cache is not working or flows are not being captured. Covers cache entry verification, timeout behaviour, flow direction validation, and traffic path confirmation. Run 'NetFlow Common Baseline' parent workflow first.
symptoms: Flow cache is empty despite traffic
Byte or packet counters stuck at zero

cli_commands.command: show flow monitor cache format table
cli_commands.purpose: Display all cache entries in table format — shows the 5-tuple, byte/packet counters, and flow timing for each active flow. Verify expected flows appear after sending test traffic

cli_commands.command: show flow monitor cache format record
cli_commands.purpose: Display cache entries in record format (verbose) — shows all match and collect fields per flow entry. Useful to verify that collect fields (bytes, packets, timestamps) are populated correctly

Display aggregated cache entries — collapses individual flow entries into aggregated summaries based on the configured aggregation keys. Useful for high-level traffic analysis and verifying aggregate counters

cli_commands.command: show flow monitor cache format record | include Current entries
cli_commands.purpose: Quick count of current active cache entries without printing full flow data — use on production devices to avoid large output
Specific monitor cache — shows cache type (normal/immediate/permanent), cache size, active/inactive timeouts, and current/high-watermark entry counts

interpretation_rules.message: Note the cache size and compare against current entries to assess utilization
interpretation_rules.recommendation: If current entries are close to cache size, increase cache entries or reduce inactive timeout to free entries faster

cli_commands.command: show flow monitor input cache format table
cli_commands.purpose: Display cache entries for input-direction monitors only — useful when separate input/output monitors are configured

cli_commands.command: show flow monitor output cache format record
cli_commands.purpose: Display cache entries for output-direction monitors — verify output monitoring is capturing egress traffic
Monitor-level statistics: flows added, flows aged (active timeout, inactive timeout, event aged, forced aged). High forced-aged count indicates cache pressure

interpretation_rules.message: Flows are being forcibly aged — cache is under pressure and flow data may be incomplete
interpretation_rules.recommendation: Increase cache entries ('cache entries <count>') or reduce inactive timeout to free entries faster

cli_commands.purpose: Verify the CEF forwarding path for test traffic — confirm traffic actually traverses the monitored interface. If the CEF entry points to a different interface, the flow monitor on the expected interface will not see the traffic

Flush all cached flow entries for the specified monitor — ALWAYS run before a new repro attempt or test to start with a clean cache. Cached entries are exported to the collector before being cleared. After clearing, send test traffic and re-check the cache to verify new flows are being created correctly

Reset the statistics counters (flows added, flows aged, active/inactive timeout expirations, forced-aged) for the specified monitor — run alongside 'clear cache' before a repro to get a clean baseline. After clearing, reproduce the issue and run 'show flow monitor stats' to see only the relevant delta counters

analysis_points: Empty cache with active traffic means the monitor is not on the correct interface/direction, or traffic is being switched on a different path (e.g., hardware-switched vs CPU-punted)
Cache entries at or near cache size indicate capacity issues — increase cache size or reduce timeouts
Forced-aged flows are exported prematurely and may have incomplete counters — this is a sign of cache pressure
Active timeout (default 1800s / 30 min) causes long-lived flows to be periodically exported and re-created. The collector should stitch these into a single logical flow
Inactive timeout (default 15s) ages out flows with no packet activity. Short-lived flows (DNS, HTTP requests) typically hit this timeout
TCP FIN/RST causes immediate flow export in normal cache mode
Verify the CEF path ensures traffic actually enters the monitored interface — routing asymmetry can cause missing flows

REPRO WORKFLOW: Always clear the cache ('clear flow monitor <name> cache') AND statistics ('clear flow monitor <name> statistics') before a repro attempt. This gives a clean baseline so delta counters reflect only the reproduced issue. Then send test traffic, then check cache and stats

runbook_fd4556a9.name: Platform FED NetFlow Diagnostics
runbook_fd4556a9.description: Platform-level NetFlow / FNF diagnostics using FED (Forwarding Engine Driver) and fwd-asic insight commands for Catalyst 9000 series switches.
